video_title: OpenAI Weighs IPO in 2027 | Bloomberg Tech 6/26/2026
gist: A Bloomberg Tech episode anchored by OpenAI signaling it may delay its IPO to 2027 (going third after SpaceX and Anthropic), set against a bruising week for the AI trade driven by a memory-chip supply squeeze that pushed Apple, Microsoft and others to raise hardware prices. It also covers SpaceX's weak debut bond, Korea's parabolic KOSPI, NVIDIA's robotics 'three computer' problem, and a Fed/affordability panel where Minneapolis Fed's Kashkari pencils in a 2026 rate HIKE and argues data-center capex, not wages, is now driving inflation.

insights.claim: OpenAI is considering an IPO as soon as 2027, a timeline that would put it public after rival Anthropic and after SpaceX, and the delay is attributed in part to current market conditions.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow / Bloomberg reporters
insights.so_what: A 2027 timeline pushes the largest AI IPO further out, removing a near-term catalyst for AI-exposed private valuations and for the underwriting banks' fee pipeline.
insights.quote: OPENAI IS CONSIDERING AN IPO AS SOON AS 2027 ACCORDING TO SOURCES. A TIMELINE THAT WOULD POTENTIALLY SEE IT GO PUBLIC AFTER ITS AI RIVAL ANTHROPIC.

insights.claim: OpenAI appears willing to let Anthropic IPO first and set the tone and valuation expectations, then watch how that listing trades before pricing its own deal.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg reporter
insights.so_what: Anthropic's pricing becomes the read-through anchor for OpenAI's eventual valuation; watch Anthropic's debut as the leading indicator for the whole AI-IPO cohort.
insights.quote: IT SEEMS LIKE THEY ARE WILLING TO CONCEDE TO ANTHROPIC GOING FIRST. THAT MEANS SETTING THE TONE AND VALUATION EXPECTATIONS.

insights.claim: Anthropic appears ahead of OpenAI on valuation, revenue growth, and the ability to generate free cash flow over the next years, despite the race being publicly framed as OpenAI-led.
insights.speaker: Anthony (Bloomberg reporter)
insights.so_what: Reframes consensus that OpenAI is the clear leader; an investor positioning across the two should weigh Anthropic's superior cash-flow trajectory.
insights.quote: THEY SEEM TO BE AHEAD OF OPENAI TERMS OF VALUATION, REVENUE GROWTH, THE ABILITY TO GENERATE FREE CASH FLOW OVER THE NEXT YEARS.

insights.claim: SoftBank shares fell sharply in Japan and again in US trading on the OpenAI IPO-delay report because SoftBank is massively economically and financially exposed to OpenAI.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow
insights.so_what: SoftBank trades as a high-beta proxy on OpenAI's listing timeline; headline risk around the IPO date moves the stock directly.
insights.quote: SOFTBANK SHARES IN THE JAPAN FELL SIGNIFICANT THE OVERNIGHT AND THE U.S. DECISION IS DOWN AGAIN TODAY BECAUSE THE NEW YORK TIMES HAD INITIALLY PUBLISHED A REPORT ABOUT OPENAI'S IPO.

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley are leading both the OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs and fell in the session on the delay news, but the move is likely a knee-jerk reaction since both banks are set for a strong fee year from equity capital markets.
insights.speaker: Anthony (Bloomberg reporter)
insights.so_what: Weakness in GS/MS on IPO-delay headlines may be a buyable overreaction given the unusually rich AI-IPO fee backlog they control.
insights.quote: THE BANKS ARE GOING TO DO VERY WELL THIS YEAR IN TERMS OF FEES FROM AREAS LIKE EQUITY CAPITAL MARKETS. THAT MIGHT JUST BE A BIT OF A TOUGH DAY.

insights.claim: Bankers are using this week's volatility as an easy excuse to hold off on IPOs, but the delay only truly matters if there is dire concern about market conditions into September, October and year-end.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg IPO reporter
insights.so_what: Treat the stated 'market conditions' rationale skeptically; the real signal is whether Q3/Q4 conditions deteriorate, not this week's chop.
insights.quote: AS LONG AS YOU CAN POINT TO THE VOLATILITY OF THIS WEEK IT IS NOT REALLY MATTER IN THE GRAND SCHEME UNLESS THERE IS SOME KIND OF DIRE CONCERN ABOUT WHAT THE MARKETS WOULD LOOK LIKE IN SEPTEMBER OR OCTOBER.

insights.claim: Apple raised prices across its hardware lineup (excluding the iPhone) due to higher memory chip costs, triggering its biggest single-day drop in roughly two months.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow
insights.so_what: Confirms the memory squeeze is now flowing into consumer-device pricing and pressuring Apple's stock; margin and demand implications ripple across the hardware supply chain.
insights.quote: APPLE IS REBOUNDING, HAVING DROPPED THE MOST AND 60 MONTHS ON THURSDAY BECAUSE IT RAISED PRICES ACROSS HARDWARE, EXCLUDING THE IPHONE.

insights.claim: Micron says memory supply will stay tight for 18 months with prices remaining elevated, and Micron itself surged after blowout earnings as it benefits from the tight supply.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow
insights.so_what: An 18-month tight-supply window underpins a durable pricing-power tailwind for memory makers like Micron while raising input-cost risk for device OEMs.
insights.quote: MACRON IS SAYING THAT MEMORY SUPPLY WILL BE TIGHT FOR 18 MONTHS. PRICES REMAIN ELEVATED.

insights.claim: The iPhone will not be spared from memory-driven price hikes; the analyst expects Pro Max pricing to rise by at least $300 and potentially as much as $200 above prior assumptions, versus an earlier baked-in assumption of a $100 hike on Pro Max and $50 on base models.
insights.speaker: Terry, senior research director (smartphone handsets)
insights.so_what: Larger-than-modeled iPhone price hikes in the fall could either protect Apple margins against memory inflation or pressure unit upgrades depending on consumer elasticity.
insights.quote: WE SAW THE MAX GOING UP TO AT LEAST $300. IT COULD GO UP AS MUCH IS $200.

insights.claim: iPhone price hikes will be concentrated strategically on Pro Max models because a $200 increase spread over a 36-month installment plan is only about $5 per month, which is unlikely to dent demand from affluent buyers.
insights.speaker: Terry, senior research director
insights.so_what: Installment-plan math means headline hikes overstate real demand impact at the high end; the elasticity risk sits in base/older models and emerging markets.
insights.quote: A $200 INCREASE REALLY AMOUNTS TO MOST PEOPLE BUYING DEVICES ON THE 36 MONTH INSTALLMENT PLAN PAIR THAT IS ONLY ABOUT A FIVE DOLLAR PRICE HIKE PER MONTH.

insights.claim: Apple is deliberately announcing iPad and Mac price hikes now, ahead of the fall iPhone launch, so consumers are psychologically prepared and the price increase does not become the launch-day story.
insights.speaker: Terry, senior research director
insights.so_what: Signals the fall iPhone reveal will carry a price increase; the staged announcements are a tell that hikes are coming, useful for timing Apple sentiment.
insights.quote: THE REASON THEY MADE THE ANNOUNCEMENTS NOW... IS SO THAT CONSUMERS GET PSYCHOLOGICALLY PREPARED. THEY DO NOT WANT THE STORY AT LAUNCH TO BE THE PRICE HIKE.

insights.claim: A large installed base of Apple devices cannot run Siri and Apple Intelligence features, creating a compelling AI-driven upgrade catalyst that should cushion demand against price hikes.
insights.speaker: Terry, senior research director
insights.so_what: AI-feature gating is an upgrade lever that could offset price-hike-driven demand erosion; watch upgrade rates as the swing factor for iPhone units.
insights.quote: MILLIONS OF DEVICES HAVE THE CAPABILITIES TO RUN APPLE INTELLIGENCE AND SIRI AI. THIS IS A SIGNIFICANT PORTION OF THEM NOT RUNNING THOSE FEATURES. THAT CREATES A COMPELLING UPGRADE REASON.

insights.claim: Microsoft announced its third Xbox console price hike in 13 months, raising one version by $100 and another by $150 effective August 1, driven by the component-shortage crisis.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow
insights.so_what: Repeated console hikes confirm the memory/component squeeze is broad across consumer electronics, not Apple-specific, pressuring hardware-margin assumptions sector-wide.
insights.quote: MICROSOFT ALSO ANNOUNCED A THIRD BIG PRICE HIKE CAN 13 MONTHS FOR XBOX CONSOLES. INCREASING BY $100. ANOTHER VERSION INCREASES BY $150.

insights.claim: Asian memory leaders Samsung and SK Hynix sold off sharply overnight as the market flipped from euphoria over tight memory pricing to fear that high prices will ultimately soften memory demand.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow
insights.so_what: The pricing-power-versus-demand-destruction debate is now the dominant driver of memory-name volatility; the market is pricing the risk that elevated prices kill the demand that justified them.
insights.quote: THE PERSPECTIVE OF THE MARKETS CHANGE. SAME SON, SK HYNIX, THE TWO LEADERS IN MEMORY DOWN SIGNIFICANTLY.

insights.claim: Samsung could announce more than $600 billion in spending over the next decade and SK Hynix will expand production to meet memory demand, to be unveiled Monday at an event hosted by South Korea's president.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow
insights.so_what: A $600B+ Samsung capex commitment is a forward catalyst and a signal that supply will eventually ramp, relevant for the medium-term memory pricing trajectory.
insights.quote: SAM SON COULD ANNOUNCE MORE THAN $600 BILLION IN SPENDING OVER THE NEXT DECADE, MARKING THE LARGEST SUCH PLANS IN THE... COMPANY'S HISTORY.

insights.claim: SpaceX's $25 billion bond offering has produced paper losses of roughly $305 million as spreads widened 15-25bp out the curve, with traders attributing the weakness to fast-money accounts looking for a quick flip.
insights.speaker: Robert Schiffman, Bloomberg senior credit analyst
insights.so_what: The weak debut is framed as a technical, fast-money flush rather than a credit-deterioration signal; the dip may be a near-term anomaly rather than a re-rating.
insights.quote: THE $25 BILLION BOND OFFERING HAS RESULTED IN PAPER LOSSES OF ROUGHLY $305 MILLION. TRADERS ARE SUGGESTING MANY FAST MONEY ACCOUNTS WERE LOOKING FOR A QUICK FLIP.

insights.claim: SpaceX is a triple-B-rated credit that trades tighter than and is rated higher than Oracle, and it is not trading like a junk company despite being a new, untracked issuer with no track record.
insights.speaker: Robert Schiffman, Bloomberg senior credit analyst
insights.so_what: Positions SpaceX paper as an investment-grade, mispriced-on-the-dip credit relative to Oracle; the near-term spread widening is viewed as transient pain, not structural.
insights.quote: THIS NAME IS RATED HIGHER THAN ORACLE. IT TREATS TIGHTER THAN ORACLE. IT DOES NOT LOOK LIKE A DRUNK COMPANY AND IS NOT TRADING LIKE A JUNK COMPANY.

insights.claim: The biggest AA/AAA tech names (Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet) are now free-cash-flow negative after capex and shareholder returns and are raising enormous amounts of capital, with Alphabet having raised $85 billion of equity a few weeks ago.
insights.speaker: Robert Schiffman, Bloomberg senior credit analyst
insights.so_what: Mega-cap tech has structurally shifted to FCF-negative funding of AI capex, meaning even AAA names are now reliant on capital markets, raising supply and refinancing dynamics for IG credit.
insights.quote: THE AA'S AND AAA'S, MICROSOFT, AMAZON, ALPHABET, ARE ACTUALLY FREE CASH FLOW NEGATIVE... ALPHABET RAISED $85 BILLION OF EQUITY JUST A FEW WEEKS AGO.

insights.claim: Recurring AI-valuation-collapse scares happen every couple of months, and this week's daily moves are overstated rather than a genuine turn in AI valuations.
insights.speaker: Robert Schiffman, Bloomberg senior credit analyst
insights.so_what: Fade-the-panic stance: an investor can treat periodic 'sky is falling' AI-credit selloffs as recurring noise unless fundamentals shift.
insights.quote: EVERYONE IS POINTING TOWARDS THE DAILY MOVES OF THE SKY IS FALLING, LOOKING FOR THE DAY THAT AI EVALUATIONS ARE GOING TO COMPLETELY TURN. WE HAVE DONE THIS DANCE BEFORE.

insights.claim: The KOSPI index has climbed roughly 200% (tripled) over the past 12 months, driven mainly by retail investors ('ants') and an AI-fueled chip rally, vastly outperforming the S&P and Nasdaq.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Originals
insights.so_what: A tripling driven by leveraged retail flow concentrated in two memory names is a textbook late-cycle bubble setup; sizing and timing the unwind is the key risk for Korea exposure.
insights.quote: THE KOSPI INDEX CLIMBED 200% OVER THE LAST 12 MONTHS... THAT MEANS IT HAS TRIPLED OVER THE PAST 12 MONTHS. RETAIL INVESTORS HAVE BEEN THE MAIN DRIVER.

insights.claim: South Korea's market is heavily reliant on just two memory chipmakers and shows signs of mania including retail investors borrowing money to invest, creating a bubble that could burst with extraordinary pain.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg Originals analyst
insights.so_what: Concentration in two names plus margin-fueled retail means a memory-demand disappointment could trigger an outsized, leveraged drawdown in Korean equities.
insights.quote: THE PAIN IN KOREA IS GOING TO BE EXTRAORDINARY.

insights.claim: Core PCE just came in at 4.1%, the highest pace since 2023 and accelerating in the wrong direction.
insights.speaker: Lisa Abramowicz
insights.so_what: Re-accelerating core inflation toward 4% materially raises the odds of a hawkish Fed path and undercuts any near-term rate-cut thesis.
insights.quote: CORE PCE WHICH JUST CAME IN AT THE HIGHEST PACE GOING BACK TO 2023... COMING IN AT FOUR POINT 1%. ACCELERATING IN THE WRONG DIRECTION.

insights.claim: Minneapolis Fed President Kashkari changed his dot-plot projection from one rate cut by year-end (in March) to one rate HIKE by year-end (in June).
insights.speaker: Neel Kashkari, Minneapolis Fed President
insights.so_what: A sitting Fed official flipping from a cut to a hike is a sharp hawkish signal for rates and duration; bond and rate-sensitive equity positioning should account for hike risk in 2026.
insights.quote: IN MARCH I HAD PENCILED IN ONE RATE CUT BY THE END OF THE YEAR. IN JUNE I'VE CHANGED THAT TO ONE RATE HIKE BY THE END OF THE YEAR.

insights.claim: Kashkari argues inflation is being driven by supply dynamics and by hundreds of billions of dollars a year flowing into data centers, not by wages, with prices skyrocketing in those specific parts of the economy.
insights.speaker: Neel Kashkari, Minneapolis Fed President
insights.so_what: If AI data-center capex is now a primary inflation driver, the Fed may need to keep policy tight to offset it, linking AI buildout directly to higher-for-longer rates.
insights.quote: IT IS ALSO BEING DRIVEN BY MASSIVE INVESTMENT, HUNDREDS OF BILLIONS OF DOLLARS A YEAR INTO DATA CENTERS... THE PRICES ARE SKYROCKETING ON THOSE PARTS OF THE ECONOMY. IT IS NOT WAGES THOUGH.

insights.claim: Kashkari says most of the Fed's analytical tools for inflation are rooted in the labor market, yet the labor market is not causing current inflation, forcing the Fed to re-examine how it thinks about what causes inflation.
insights.speaker: Neel Kashkari, Minneapolis Fed President
insights.so_what: A Fed admitting its core analytical framework is mismatched to the current supply/capex-driven inflation implies higher policy-error risk and less predictable reaction functions.
insights.quote: MOST OF THE ANALYTICAL TOOLS WE HAVE TO ANALYZE INFLATION STARRED IN THE LABOR MARKET. YET THE LABOR MARKET IS NOT CAUSING THE INFLATION.

insights.claim: Kashkari expects wages to keep climbing as workers eventually demand to catch up to inflation, which would cause inflation to fall less quickly than it otherwise would even if supply dynamics fade.
insights.speaker: Neel Kashkari, Minneapolis Fed President
insights.so_what: A delayed wage-catch-up channel argues for stickier inflation and a slower disinflation glide path, reinforcing the higher-for-longer rate scenario.
insights.quote: EVEN IF THE SUPPLY DYNAMICS FADE I WOULD EXPECT WAGES TO CONTINUE CLIMBING... IT MAY CAUSE INFLATION TO FALL A LITTLE LESS QUICKLY THAN IT OTHERWISE WOULD.

insights.claim: Kashkari distrusts Iran honoring its agreement and cites overnight evidence they are already reneging, keeping him cautious that Middle East energy-driven inflation risk is not resolved.
insights.speaker: Neel Kashkari, Minneapolis Fed President
insights.so_what: Geopolitical energy risk via the Strait of Hormuz remains a live upside-inflation tail that keeps the Fed biased hawkish; watch oil and Iran headlines as policy inputs.
insights.quote: I DON'T TRUST IRAN TO HONOR WHATEVER AGREEMENT HAS BEEN MADE. THERE IS SOME EVIDENCE OVERNIGHT THAT THEY ARE ALREADY RENEGING ON IT.

themes.theme: K-shaped economy and AI labor disruption

insights.claim: More than half of US grocery customers no longer have a primary grocer and shop three or four retailers a week buying in real-time rather than stocking up, reflecting deal-hunting and food-waste avoidance under cost pressure.
insights.speaker: Amazon grocery executive
insights.so_what: A structural shift to fragmented, value-driven grocery shopping pressures retailer loyalty and basket economics, signaling real consumer strain at the lower end.
insights.quote: OVER HALF OF CUSTOMERS ARE SHOPPING MANY DIFFERENT RETAILERS, THREE OR FOUR A WEEK... TO FIND THE BEST DEALS AND VALUES.

insights.claim: Lyft has more drivers on its platform than ever in history, which the CEO attributes to people needing supplemental income, with 85% driving part-time as a second source of income.
insights.speaker: David (Lyft CEO)
insights.so_what: Record gig-driver supply driven by income shortfalls is a real-economy signal of household financial stress, useful as a ground-level read on consumer health.
insights.quote: WE HAVE MORE DRIVERS ON THE LIFT PLATFORM THAN EVER IN HISTORY... 85% OF PEOPLE ARE DRIVING PART-TIME AND DOING IT A SUPPLEMENTAL INCOME.

insights.claim: Betsy warns of a possible contagion from AI displacing narrow data-science and coding roles to a much broader set of white-collar jobs, hitting young people first.
insights.speaker: Betsy (panelist)
insights.so_what: If AI white-collar displacement broadens, it pressures consumer income and could weaken the very demand AI-capex bulls are counting on; an underappreciated second-order risk.
insights.quote: THERE IS WORRY THERE IS GOING TO BE A CONTAGION TO A MUCH BROADER SET OF WHITE-COLLAR JOBS AND IT IS GOING TO IMPACT YOUNG PEOPLE FIRST.

insights.claim: Kashkari notes the labor market has cooled to 'decent' and mostly moving sideways, but mobility has collapsed so workers who need higher pay cannot find openings, fueling broad consumer dissatisfaction.
insights.speaker: Neel Kashkari, Minneapolis Fed President
insights.so_what: A 'sideways' labor market with low mobility supports neither a clear easing nor a recession call, keeping the Fed and consumer outlook ambiguous.
insights.quote: I WOULD CALL IT A DECENT LABOR MARKET MOSTLY MOVING SIDEWAYS, KIND OF TREADING WATER. IT IS NOT IN A BAD PLACE OR A GREAT PLACE EITHER.

themes.theme: NVIDIA robotics and the AI-brain gap

insights.claim: NVIDIA says humanoid robot hardware is largely ready but the industry is still hunting for a breakthrough in AI 'brains,' and the field's ChatGPT-like moment is close but has not yet arrived.
insights.speaker: NVIDIA VP of Robotics and Edge
insights.so_what: The bottleneck for the humanoid-robot wave is software/general-purpose accuracy, not hardware; investors should watch for the model breakthrough as the real inflection catalyst.
insights.quote: WHAT HAPPENED WITH CHATGPT IN 2022 YOU HAD THAT MOMENT, THAT MOMENT IS RIGHT AROUND THE CORNER BUT WE HAVE NOT REACHED IT YET.

insights.claim: NVIDIA frames robotics around a 'three computer problem' (training, simulation/testing via Omniverse, and the in-robot brain), with over 2.5 million developers active and more than 10,000 companies building robots on its platform.
insights.speaker: NVIDIA VP of Robotics and Edge
insights.so_what: NVIDIA is building an end-to-end robotics platform moat (2.5M developers, 10k companies) that extends its AI franchise into physical embodiment, a long-duration TAM expansion.
insights.quote: OVER 2.5 MILLION DEVELOPERS ARE ACTIVE ON THE PLATFORM. MORE THAN 10,000 COMPANIES ARE BUILDING ALL SORTS OF ROBOTS.

insights.claim: Disney is investing $60 billion over a 10-year plan in its parks and resorts, including a major robotics upgrade with dolphin-like robots and projection-based effects driven by new CEO Josh D'Amaro.
insights.speaker: Ed Ludlow
insights.so_what: Disney's $60B parks capex anchored in robotics is a concrete demand signal for the embodied-AI/robotics supply chain and a strategic bet by its new CEO.
insights.quote: DISNEY'S 10-YEAR PLAN TO INVEST $60 BILLION IN ITS PARKS AND RESORTS, INCLUDING A FLEET OF DOLPHIN-LIKE ROBOTS.
